Activity Name: Welcome to Career Catalyst Week 1
Notes: Provides an overview of what will be covered this week, objectives, suggested order of completion and time estimates.

Activity Name: What Is Experiential Learning?
Notes: Frames the learning cycle and sets expectations for how this course differs from traditional coursework

Activity Name: Syllabus and assessment review
Notes: Foundational for academic integrity and student planning. Students look ahead to what is expected and how they will be assessed in the course.

Activity Name: AI in Today's Workplace & This Course
Notes: Introduces the AI-enabled workplace context and ties it to the course's policy orientation: what's permitted, what requires disclosure, the defend-your-work standard

Activity Name: Your Project and Team
Notes: Orients students to the project-based structure and how they will be expected to work in teams

Activity Name: Platform orientation
Notes: Navigating Riipen and LMS; reduces friction in subsequent weeks

Activity Name: Review project briefs
Notes: Students browse all available briefs before deep-reading; skimming first prevents anchoring on the first brief encountered; students select finalists based on initial skim and read carefully before forming a preference

Activity Name: Complete student intake, including preference submission with written rationale
Notes: Frames the purpose of the intake questionnaire, how it will support project matching, and should be used for reflection and goal-tracking. Written justification for project selections required; develops reasoned decision-making and surfaces student priorities.

Activity Name: LMS introduction post + reading peers
Notes: Community-building before teams are assigned; students post a self-intro and respond to peers

Activity Name: Module: Introduction to Reflective Practice and the DEAL Model
Notes: Introduces reflective practice as a professional skill; teaches the three DEAL stages (Describe, Examine, Articulate Learning) with a worked example; students will apply the framework for the first time in Week 3

Activity Name: Setting Yourself Up for Success
Notes: Time management and planning basics for project-based work; early semester planning is a reliable predictor of student performance

Activity Name: Course calendar / planning
Notes: Students map deliverable dates to their own calendars; supports time management and reduces late submissions

Activity Name: Professional Email Basics
Notes: Practical skill introduced before students need to communicate professionally; applies immediately in Week 2 outreach

Activity Name: Team norms overview
Notes: Pre-exposure to Week 2 content before teams are assigned; students arrive primed for the charter conversation

Activity Name: Team Charter template review
Notes: Template review only — not completed this week; reduces cognitive load when teams meet in Week 2

Activity Name: Week 2 Overview — objectives, what's happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: Sets expectations for the week; names anchor events (first team meeting, employer outreach email); gives suggested order of completion. Parallel to 1.01 in Week 1.

Activity Name: Using AI Responsibly module section
Notes: How to prompt well, how to evaluate AI output critically, how to verify claims, how to use AI as a thinking partner rather than an answer machine; includes example prompts and critical evaluation exercises; first formal introduction of prompt strategy

Activity Name: Setting Your Team Up for Success
Notes: Three things to get agreement on in your first meeting: how you'll communicate, how you'll make decisions, what 'done' looks like for your team. A brief framing of what the Team Charter is actually for — not a compliance document, but a reference point you'll actually use when things get bumpy. Moved earlier in module sequence — team formation content before kickoff prep content.

Activity Name: Review teammates' LMS intro posts
Notes: Now that team assignments are known, students read their teammates' Week 1 intro posts; community-building with the specific people they'll be working with for 15 weeks

Activity Name: First contact with teammates + scheduling
Notes: Initiating contact, finding a meeting time across four schedules; inherently inefficient for strangers; async coordination via email or LMS messaging

Activity Name: First team meeting
Notes: 90-minute synchronous meeting covering introductions, shared read-through of the project brief, initial reactions, and charter negotiation

Activity Name: Team Charter drafting, review, and finalization
Notes: All four students must agree on the substance before the charter is complete

Activity Name: Async team communication across the week
Notes: Coordination outside of the meeting; Slack, email, or LMS; lighter than subsequent weeks because the relationship is brand new and norms are still forming

Activity Name: Draft and agree on employer outreach email
Notes: Team drafts the introduction email and requests the kickoff meeting; collective agreement required before sending; AI-assisted drafting is permitted with disclosure

Activity Name: Employer scheduling coordination
Notes: Follow-up to confirm receipt and handle logistics; brief but real; missing from original time estimate

Activity Name: Researching an Organization — organizational reconnaissance primer
Notes: Scoped to Week 2 task: show up to the kickoff informed. Who is this company, what do they do, what's their sector, any recent news. Shallow and broad — not analytical. Part of the research skills progression: Week 2 = reconnaissance, Week 3 = targeted landscape, Week 4 = analytical synthesis.

Activity Name: Kickoff Best Practices
Notes: Just-in-time practical guidance specific to the kickoff meeting: role assignment framework (who asks, who notes, who manages time), how to open the meeting, what to listen for, what you're trying to walk away with, what to do if the conversation goes off track. Distinct from Asking Good Questions which teaches transferable skills — this section is operationally specific to this meeting.

Activity Name: Asking Good Questions
Notes: Transferable skill applicable throughout the course (Direction Check, Progress Reviews, etc.). Open vs. closed questions, how to prioritize questions, how to listen for what's not being said. Taught as a durable skill, not kickoff-specific — Kickoff Best Practices handles the operational specifics.

Activity Name: Active Listening in Client Meetings
Notes: How to listen without waiting to talk, note-taking strategies, clarifying without interrupting. Transferable skill that applies to all employer touchpoints throughout the course.

Activity Name: Review Context Analysis & Project Plan assignment + rubric
Notes: Knowing the assessment criteria before beginning work shapes what they prioritize in research and charter conversations

Activity Name: Individual research on employer organization
Notes: Students now have a real project assignment and genuine motivation to understand their context; feeds Context Analysis in Week 4

Activity Name: Week 3 Overview — objectives, what's happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: Sets expectations for the week; names the anchor event (employer kickoff meeting) and deliverable (Kickoff Summary); gives suggested order of completion.

Activity Name: Using AI for Note Capture and Summarization
Notes: How to use AI transcription or summarization tools during or immediately after the kickoff meeting; how to prompt AI to organize raw notes into a structured summary; introduced before the meeting so students arrive ready to use it.

Activity Name: Professional Video Call Etiquette
Notes: Camera, lighting, background, muting protocols; how to handle technical issues without derailing the meeting; managing note-taking while staying engaged on camera; what to do when people talk over each other. First professional video call with an employer — practical and specific.

Activity Name: Pre-meeting prep — review Week 2 research, organize and prioritize questions, assign meeting roles
Notes: Students review their individual research from Week 2, consolidate questions as a team, prioritize by importance, and assign roles (who asks, who takes notes, who manages time). Async individual work feeding into the team huddle.

Activity Name: Team huddle — quick run-through before the kickoff
Notes: Short synchronous meeting to align on questions, confirm roles, and do a brief rehearsal. Distinct from pre-meeting prep (which is individual/async) — this is the team getting on the same page immediately before the employer meeting.

Activity Name: Email exchange with employer — confirm logistics, pre-meeting questions
Notes: Confirming meeting logistics, any platform/tech setup, and possibly one or two clarifying questions before the kickoff. Professional correspondence applying Week 1 email skills in a client context.

Activity Name: Employer kickoff meeting
Notes: Anchor event of the week. 60-min hold (may end early). Employer presents organizational context, project scope, success criteria, timeline, key contacts; students listen and ask prepared questions. First direct employer interaction. Students are not presenting — this is a listening and orienting touchpoint. All employer meetings follow a consistent 60-min hold, biweekly cadence starting this week.

Activity Name: Immediate post-meeting debrief
Notes: Quick synchronous or async debrief immediately after the kickoff while impressions are fresh. What surprised us, what we need to find out, what the employer seemed to care most about. Captures reactions before memory fades — distinct from the formal Kickoff Summary.

Activity Name: Processing What You Heard — turning notes into shared understanding
Notes: How to turn raw meeting notes into shared team understanding; identifying what you learned vs. what's still unclear; reconciling different team members' interpretations of what was said. Just-in-time before Kickoff Summary drafting.

Activity Name: Following Up Professionally — the post-meeting email
Notes: How to write a post-meeting follow-up email to the employer: confirming understanding, thanking them for their time, flagging any open questions. Applies Week 1 email skills in a specific post-kickoff context.

Activity Name: Identifying Gaps and Open Questions
Notes: What don't you know yet? What assumptions are you making? How to distinguish between confirmed information and open questions. Feeds directly into the Kickoff Summary format and the post-kickoff research.

Activity Name: Kickoff Summary — format, purpose, and what good looks like
Notes: What the Kickoff Summary is for: a factual record of the employer's stated expectations, not analysis or interpretation. What to include (confirmed scope, deliverables, success criteria, timeline, key contacts, open questions). How to distinguish confirmed information from assumptions. Completion criteria reviewed before drafting.

Activity Name: Draft, review, and submit Kickoff Summary
Notes: One team member drafts; all members review and agree on accuracy before submission. 120 min reflects the full cycle: drafting, async back-and-forth, revisions, and final submission. AI-assisted organization permitted with disclosure; the content must reflect what was actually said.

Activity Name: Researching Your Client's Context — targeted landscape primer
Notes: Part 2 of the research skills progression (Week 2 = reconnaissance, Week 3 = targeted landscape, Week 4 = analytical synthesis). Now that students know the actual project scope from the kickoff, they can research with direction. Where to look, what you're trying to understand, how to organize findings before you can synthesize them.

Activity Name: Post-kickoff research — targeted landscape orientation
Notes: Students research the competitive landscape and sector context relevant to their specific project scope. More targeted than Week 2 reconnaissance because scope is now confirmed. Feeds directly into Week 4 Context Analysis.

Activity Name: Looking Ahead — Introduction to the Project Plan
Notes: Just-in-time preview before Week 4's graded deliverable. What a project plan is for, what goes in it, how it connects to the Context Analysis. Not full instruction — forward preparation only so students arrive in Week 4 primed.

Activity Name: Async team communication + admin across the week
Notes: Coordination outside of synchronous meetings; platform logistics; any follow-up admin tasks. Higher than Week 2 async because the kickoff meeting generates more coordination needs.

Activity Name: Reflection #1 — draft and submit (DEAL model)
Notes: Graded individual submission (~5% of final grade); students draw from Foundation phase experience (Weeks 1–3); first DEAL reflection in the course — include DEAL reference sheet and pro tip on using AI for reflection within the assignment prompt; sequenced after kickoff so students have a significant experience to draw from; submitted via Riipen platform; AI disclosure required; 90 min standardized across all reflections

Activity Name: Week 4 Overview — objectives, what's happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: Sets up the week; anchor deliverable is Context Analysis & Project Plan; names the cognitive shift from gathering to arguing

Activity Name: Synthesizing Research into Insights
Notes: Moving from "what we found" to "what it means" — patterns, conclusions, avoiding summary traps

Activity Name: Evidence-Based Reasoning
Notes: What counts as evidence; supporting claims vs. asserting them; acknowledging uncertainty

Activity Name: From Insights to Strategic Implications
Notes: The "so what" move — naming assumptions, identifying constraints, connecting research to project approach

Activity Name: Prioritization in Planning
Notes: Scoping to reality, making tradeoffs explicit, what to do and what not to do

Activity Name: Risk Identification and Mitigation
Notes: What goes wrong in projects; likelihood vs. impact thinking; realistic mitigation

Activity Name: Using AI for Research and Synthesis
Notes: Prompting AI for competitive landscape or synthesis; evaluating output; verifying claims; what AI cannot do

Activity Name: Deep research — competitive landscape, industry trends, stakeholder context
Notes: Students divide research responsibilities; heaviest single project block so far

Activity Name: Independent research on unfamiliar methods
Notes: Self-directed skill acquisition — how to do competitive analysis, stakeholder mapping, risk matrices

Activity Name: Team synthesis meeting — align on key findings and strategic direction
Notes: Team shares individual research, identifies most important insights, agrees on strategic framing before drafting begins

Activity Name: Individual drafting of assigned sections
Notes: Each team member drafts their portion of the Context Analysis & Project Plan

Activity Name: Async review of teammates' sections + comments
Notes: Cross-review before integration pass — gaps, inconsistencies, disconnects between sections

Activity Name: Integration pass — making it read as one document
Notes: Unified voice, transitions, no contradictions; one person leads or shared effort

Activity Name: Final team review and sign-off
Notes: Final check before submission; everyone affirms the document

Activity Name: Submit Context Analysis & Project Plan via LMS
Async team communication across the week
Notes: Higher than Week 3 due to more handoffs across research, drafting, and integration

Activity Name: Week 5 Overview — objectives, what's happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: Names the Direction Check as the anchor event; orients students to the pre/during/post structure of the week

Activity Name: Module: Communicating Your Approach
Notes: Presenting a plan for validation, framing recommendations, inviting feedback

Activity Name: Module: Managing Expectations
Notes: What to do when scope shifts, delivering difficult messages, saying "we can't do that" professionally

Activity Name: Module: Preparing for Direction Check
Notes: What to present, what questions to ask, how to use this touchpoint effectively; includes contingency guidance for when the meeting doesn't go as expected — e.g., employer hasn't read the submission, scope shifts unexpectedly, or the conversation goes off-agenda

Activity Name: Module: Receiving Direction and Responding Well
Notes: How to listen without defending, asking clarifying questions when feedback is vague, translating verbal feedback into written plan adjustments

Activity Name: Review Context Analysis feedback + prep for Direction Check (team meeting)
Notes: Team reviews graded feedback on Context Analysis & Project Plan, if available; identifies what employer is likely to probe; assigns talking points

Activity Name: Prepare talking points for Direction Check
Notes: Individual + team drafting of what to present, how to frame proposed approach, and what questions to ask; includes team review before meeting

Activity Name: Email coordination with employer — confirm logistics
Notes: Confirming meeting link, time, attendees; professional communication practice

Activity Name: Direction Check — present Context Analysis and Project Plan, receive employer feedback on approach
Notes: 60-min hold (may end early). First of only two formal student presentations in the course (the other is the Final Presentation in Week 15). Students present key findings from context research (15 min), proposed approach and rationale (10 min); employer feedback on direction (15 min); discussion and refinement (10 min); confirm priorities and next steps (5 min). Critical decision point where employer validates or redirects the project before students commit to production.

Activity Name: Post-meeting debrief — what did we hear, what changes?
Notes: Immediate team debrief while conversation is fresh; captures verbal feedback before it degrades; precedes written summary

Activity Name: Draft Direction Check Summary
Notes: Translates verbal employer feedback into the written checkpoint document (1 page); first draft only

Activity Name: Async review of Direction Check Summary + finalize and submit
Notes: Team reviews draft asynchronously, consolidates edits, and submits; completion-based checkpoint

Activity Name: Revise project plan based on Direction Check feedback
Notes: Updates approach, milestones, or scope based on employer feedback; connects feedback to concrete planning decisions

Activity Name: Develop deliverable structures — report outline + deck skeleton, divide sections
Notes: Translates revised approach into structural scaffolding for both final deliverables; divides drafting sections; sets up Week 6+ production

Activity Name: Independent learning — research skills, formats, or domain knowledge needed for project production
Notes: Self-directed learning based on each student's own gaps and needs; may include understanding unfamiliar deliverable formats, building domain knowledge, or developing skills needed for the production work ahead; varies by project type and student background

Activity Name: Async team communication across the week
Notes: Coordination, logistics, and check-ins outside structured activities

Activity Name: Week 6 Overview — objectives, what's happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: Names Reflection #1 as the anchor deliverable; orients students to the conceptual + production structure of the week

Activity Name: Module: Making Progress Under Uncertainty
Notes: How to draft with incomplete information, when to keep researching vs. start writing, how to flag a blocker without stalling the team

Activity Name: Module: From Research to Draft — Organizing Notes, Building Outlines, and Moving into Production
Notes: How to cluster and synthesize raw research notes into an organized outline; how to evaluate whether an outline is ready to draft from; how to move from outline to prose without freezing; AI use cases for this process including organizing notes, stress-testing outline structure, and identifying gaps before drafting begins

Activity Name: Module: Navigating Disagreement with Teammates and Clients
Notes: Disagreeing productively during production; when to defer to employer direction vs. push back gracefully; connects to Direction Check feedback students just received

Activity Name: Module: Formatting Professional Documents
Notes: Practical standards for professional document presentation: visual hierarchy, formatting consistency across team-authored sections, headings and white space, consistent terminology and style, basic visual elements (tables, charts, callouts); taught at the start of the first substantive production week so students build good habits from the beginning rather than retroactively fixing formatting in Week 11; the internal review gate (introduced Week 9) will catch formatting issues, but this module prevents the worst habits from forming; connects to the Deliverable Quality dimension on the Final Deliverables rubric (30% of grade) and the Communication dimension (15%)

Activity Name: Independent research to fill project knowledge gaps
Notes: Self-directed research to address gaps surfaced during deliverable structure work in Week 5

Activity Name: Project work — research, drafting, outlining, and section development
Notes: First substantive production week; students work on their assigned deliverable sections; may include continued research, outlining, note-taking, and drafting depending on project type and individual assignment; front-loaded to take advantage of available time before Week 7 WIP presentation prep

Activity Name: Team sync — drafting check-in, surface blockers, align on progress
Notes: Internal sync; no employer touchpoint this week; longer than a check-in to accommodate first production week coordination

Activity Name: Async team communication across the week
Notes: Coordination, logistics, check-ins outside structured activities; contribution record relevant to Peer Evaluation #1 in Week 8

Activity Name: Reflection #2 — draft and submit (DEAL model)
Notes: Graded individual submission (~5% of final grade); students draw from Discovery phase experience (Weeks 4–6); second DEAL reflection — framework no longer new, expectation shifts to depth and honesty of examination; AI-as-Socratic-partner pro tip embedded in assignment; AI disclosure required; submitted via Riipen; 90 min standardized across all reflections

Activity Name: Week 7 Overview — objectives, what's happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: Names Check-In #1 as the anchor event; orients students to the production-centric module structure and feedback cycle; first week of Development phase; this is a questions-based employer meeting, not a formal presentation

Activity Name: Module: Receiving Feedback Well
Notes: Compressed from 20 min; 'Why Feedback Feels Hard' framing folded in as brief intro rather than standalone section; Listen → Clarify → Acknowledge → Decide Later protocol; just-in-time for Check-In #1

Activity Name: Module: From Plan to Product — What Professional Deliverables Look Like
Notes: NEW — Conventions of client-facing reports, decks, and memos; difference between academic writing and professional writing; what makes a document 'polished' vs. 'complete'; fills the gap left by self-directed format research in Week 5

Activity Name: Module: Reviewing Your Teammates' Work
Notes: Reframed from abstract 'Giving Useful Feedback' to concrete production review skill; how to read a colleague's draft section for clarity, argument structure, evidence quality, and tone; distinguishes line editing from structural feedback; positions as Peer Eval #1 prep for Week 8

Activity Name: Module: Working with Data and Visuals in Your Deliverables
Notes: NEW — When to use tables, charts, or narrative; how to present numbers so they support your argument; using AI to help visualize data while maintaining accuracy; keeping it simple for a client audience

Activity Name: Module: Preparing for Check-In #1
Notes: How to prepare for a questions-based employer meeting rather than a formal presentation; developing specific questions and decision points that make the meeting productive; anticipating what the employer might ask; planning how to capture and process input; this meeting is about getting what you need from the employer, not performing progress

Activity Name: Review Work-in-Progress Summary assignment + draft summary — progress update, challenges, and questions for employer
Notes: Students first encounter the WIP Summary assignment description and completion criteria (not previewed in Week 6), then draft the document (1–2 pages): completed work, current status, challenges encountered, specific feedback needs; checkpoint deliverable; forces teams to articulate progress clearly before the meeting

Activity Name: Team prep session — develop questions and decision points for Check-In #1, plan agenda
Notes: Internal preparation for Check-In #1; no presentation rehearsal needed — this is a questions-based meeting; teams identify specific questions, decision points, and areas where employer input would unblock progress; prepare brief status update (what's done, what's in progress, what's stuck); assign who leads which topic; emphasis on making the meeting productive rather than performing progress

Activity Name: Check-In #1 — bring questions and decision points, employer as resource
Notes: 60-min hold (may end early). No formal presentation — students drive the agenda with specific questions, decision points, and areas where they need employer input. Brief status update (5 min), student questions and decision points (35–40 min), employer observations or emerging concerns (10 min), confirm priorities for next two weeks (5 min). Students' time is better spent doing the work than preparing a presentation about the work. If the team has no pressing questions, the meeting ends early.

Activity Name: Post-meeting debrief — capture answers and decisions from Check-In #1, identify action items
Notes: Immediate team debrief while conversation is fresh; captures employer answers to questions, decisions made, and any new information surfaced; translates verbal input into written record and concrete next steps; parallels Week 5 post-Direction-Check debrief pattern

Activity Name: Independent learning — research, skills, or domain knowledge to address gaps surfaced during production
Notes: Self-directed learning on targeted topics based on each student's own gaps; may include deepening domain knowledge, learning unfamiliar methods, or building skills needed for revision work ahead; consistent with Weeks 5 and 6 pattern

Activity Name: Continue project production — drafting, revision, and section development
Notes: Core production continues; students incorporate early takeaways from Check-In #1 where possible; reduced from 180 to 170 min to absorb module time increase; still substantial block reflecting production-intensive Development phase

Activity Name: Async team communication across the week
Notes: Coordination, logistics, check-ins outside structured activities; includes new standing prompt: Weekly Progress Check-In (first instance — teams check against project plan: what's done, what moved, what's blocked, who owns what this week; continues through Week 13); contribution record relevant to Peer Evaluation #1 in Week 8

Activity Name: Week 8 Overview — objectives, what’s happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: Names Peer Evaluation #1 as the anchor deliverable; orients students to the feedback-processing + peer eval structure of the week; no employer touchpoint this week

Activity Name: Module: Processing the Feedback You Received
Notes: Sorting employer feedback from Check-In #1: what to act on, what to note, what to set aside; making a feedback response plan; distinguishes between feedback that requires revision, feedback that informs future direction, and feedback to acknowledge but set aside

Activity Name: Module: Iteration as a Skill
Notes: Revision isn’t failure — it’s the professional process; how to move from feedback to concrete revisions; connects to Responsiveness to Feedback dimension on Final Deliverables rubric

Activity Name: Module: Writing Substantive Peer Evaluations
Notes: Continue/Start/Adjust qualitative framework; BARS anchors and how to use them; distinction between vague ratings and actionable evidence with examples; calibration exercise using Maple & Main scenario (rate fictional teammate Jordan using BARS, guided discussion of correct ratings); how to be honest without being destructive; directly feeds Peer Eval #1 due this week

Activity Name: Module: AI as a Production Partner
Notes: Using AI for drafting, structural review, gap identification, self-editing during production; where AI helps vs. where it substitutes for thinking; maintaining ownership of AI-assisted work; connects to disclosure requirements on upcoming deliverables

Activity Name: Review Peer Evaluation #1 assignment, BARS rubric, and evidence expectations
Notes: Students read the peer eval assignment description; review the four BARS dimensions (Reliability, Contribution, Constructive Engagement, Accountability) with behavioral anchors; understand the evidence requirement and self-evaluation component; first encounter with this deliverable format

Activity Name: Draft and submit Peer Evaluation #1 — qualitative feedback + BARS ratings + self-evaluation for each teammate
Notes: GRADED individual deliverable; four-part evaluation per teammate: (A) Continue/Start/Adjust qualitative feedback with 2-sentence minimum per prompt, (B) BARS ratings on four dimensions (Reliability, Contribution, Constructive Engagement, Accountability) with behavioral anchors and required evidence, (C) self-evaluation on same BARS dimensions, (D) team pulse questions (instructor-only); AI disclosure required; evidence without behavioral specifics flagged for instructor review

Activity Name: Team feedback response planning — prioritize employer feedback from Check-In #1, assign revision responsibilities, update project plan
Notes: Translates Check-In #1 debrief notes into a prioritized revision plan; which feedback to act on first, who owns each revision, how the timeline shifts; first structured application of 'Processing the Feedback You Received' module content; seeds Change Log habit

Activity Name: Continue project production — revisions based on Check-In #1 feedback + continued drafting and section development
Notes: Largest production block in the course so far; students simultaneously revising based on employer feedback and continuing forward production; some revisions may require rethinking approach not just editing; time is generous because this week has no employer meeting consuming synchronous time

Activity Name: Independent learning — research, skills, or domain knowledge to address gaps surfaced by Check-In #1 feedback or production challenges
Notes: Self-directed learning; Check-In #1 feedback may have surfaced domain gaps or skill needs students hadn't anticipated; consistent with Weeks 5–7 pattern

Activity Name: Async team communication across the week
Notes: Coordination, logistics, check-ins; continues Weekly Progress Check-In standing prompt (second instance); introduces Status Update to Employer standing prompt (brief async update between employer check-ins); contribution record now directly relevant given Peer Evaluation just completed

Activity Name: Week 9 Overview — objectives, what's happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: Names Draft Deliverable checkpoint and Check-In #2 as dual anchors; orients students to the production + employer meeting structure; this is a questions-based employer meeting, not a formal presentation; flags that peer eval results and employer mid-project survey results are available and will be discussed in the Team Processing Session

Activity Name: Module: Making Your Sections Talk to Each Other
Notes: The #1 problem with team-produced documents: they read like separate papers stapled together; how to unify voice, create transitions, ensure logical flow across sections written by different people; directly feeds the Draft Deliverable checkpoint and the Communication dimension on the Final Deliverables rubric

Activity Name: Module: From "Done" to "Useful" — The Actionability Test
Notes: Students tend to produce work that answers 'what did we find?' without answering 'what should the employer do about it?'; how to evaluate your own draft for actionability; maps directly to Actionability dimension on Final Deliverables rubric (20% of final grade); teaching it now while students still have time to restructure; includes AI prompt strategy: using AI to stress-test recommendations for actionability (reinforces Week 8 AI-as-production-partner concept in a new application)

Activity Name: Module: Scope Decisions — What to Keep, What to Defer
Notes: Around the midpoint, scope creeps; how to make deliberate decisions: add and deprioritize, or defer to 'future recommendations'; how to say 'that's a great idea and it's outside our timeline' professionally; feeds Actionability dimension and professional communication

Activity Name: Module: Having the Peer Evaluation Conversation
Notes: Students received anonymized results from Peer Evaluation #1 and employer mid-project survey; how to bring up results with your team, how to respond if feedback surprises you, how to move from feedback to concrete behavioral agreements; practical protocol; prepares students for the Team Processing Session (9.06)

Activity Name: Team Processing Session — discuss peer evaluation results and employer mid-project evaluation, agree on specific behavioral changes
Notes: Structured activity; teams process three feedback sources: anonymized peer eval qualitative feedback (Continue/Start/Adjust), aggregated BARS averages, and employer mid-project survey results; produce a brief written agreement documenting specific behavioral changes; agreement appended to Draft Deliverable submission; creates reference point for Peer Evaluation #2 in Week 15

Activity Name: Internal review gate — team reads assembled draft as a complete document before employer sees it
Notes: First instance of internal review gate (recurs before each subsequent checkpoint/graded submission); teams read the full draft end-to-end, not just their own sections; identify where sections don't connect, where arguments are weak; applies 9.02 module content directly; professional quality practice

Activity Name: Team prep session — develop questions and sticking points for Check-In #2, plan agenda
Notes: Internal preparation for Check-In #2; no presentation rehearsal needed — same questions-based format as Week 7; teams identify specific questions informed by production experience, peer evaluation feedback, and internal review findings; prepare brief status update; by Week 9, teams should be more practiced at preparing productive agendas for employer meetings

Activity Name: Check-In #2 — bring questions and sticking points, employer as resource
Notes: 60-min hold (may end early). No formal presentation — same questions-based format as Check-In #1 (Week 7). Brief status update (5 min), student questions covering production challenges, scope decisions, and quality concerns (35–40 min), employer observations (10 min), preview of what the near-final package will include (5 min). Students have now been through a peer evaluation cycle and draft deliverable process, so questions may be more refined than Week 7.

Activity Name: Post-meeting debrief — capture answers and decisions from Check-In #2, identify action items
Notes: Immediate team debrief while conversation is fresh; parallels Week 7 debrief and Week 5 debrief pattern; captures employer answers to questions, decisions made, and any new information; translates verbal input into written record and concrete next steps; seeds Change Log habit (formal intro in Week 10)

Activity Name: Finalize and submit Draft Deliverable checkpoint (including team processing agreement as appendix)
Notes: Team submits current draft with appended team processing agreement from 9.06; completion-based checkpoint; draft shows substantive progress and represents genuine work product, not an outline or placeholder; AI disclosure required

Activity Name: Continue project production — revisions, integration, and section development
Notes: Core production block; students apply PR#2 feedback where possible, continue integrating sections, address gaps identified in internal review gate (9.07); smaller than Week 8's production block (210 min) due to employer meeting and team processing consuming significant time

Activity Name: Async team communication across the week
Notes: Coordination, logistics, check-ins outside structured activities; includes standing prompts: Weekly Progress Check-In (continues from Week 7) and Status Update to Employer (brief between-reviews update if appropriate); contribution record relevant to Professional Engagement

Activity Name: Module: DEAL Reflection — Going Deeper
Notes: Third time students use DEAL (after Weeks 3 and 6); the framework isn't new — the challenge is depth; moving beyond describe what happened to examining assumptions, patterns, and professional implications; concrete techniques for strengthening the Examine stage; just-in-time scaffolding before Reflection #3 due this week

Activity Name: Reflection #3 — draft and submit (DEAL model)
Notes: Third DEAL reflection; draws from Weeks 7-9 (most intensive feedback and production period); focus: a specific moment involving feedback or collaboration; 90 min reflects higher depth expectation; AI-as-Socratic-partner prompt embedded in assignment (reinforced from Week 3); submitted via Riipen; AI-assisted drafting off-limits for reflections

Activity Name: Week 10 Overview — objectives, what's happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: Final week of Development phase; no graded deliverable this week — Reflection #3 was submitted in Week 9; orients to the shift from building sections to assembling a coherent package; flags that Week 11 opens Refinement with the Near-Final Review and the Near-Final Package checkpoint; NOTE: This week is intentionally lighter than Week 9 (~7 hrs vs ~9 hrs). After the most intensive week in the course (PR#2, Team Processing, Draft Deliverable, Reflection #3), students need room to regroup and focus on integration work. The lighter load is recovery by design, not a scheduling gap.

Activity Name: Module: What Are You Actually Learning?
Notes: Surfacing tacit learning — naming skills developing that weren't planned for; connecting daily project work to competencies; students are 10 weeks in and often can't articulate what they've gained beyond we made a deliverable; grounds the reflection by helping students see experience as a source of professional insight

Activity Name: Module: From Experience to Concept — Making Learning Transferable
Notes: How to extract principles from specific situations; the difference between I learned to use Excel and I learned how to translate ambiguous client requests into a research plan; what makes learning transferable vs. situation-specific; directly feeds the Articulation of Learning dimension in the reflection rubric

Activity Name: Module: Introducing the Change Log
Notes: Formal introduction of the Change Log — a running record of feedback received, how it was addressed, and rationale for feedback not incorporated; includes 'what good entries look like' section with examples of strong vs. weak log entries; students have been informally tracking feedback since Week 7 debriefs; Change Log is required component of Final Deliverables (Week 13); introducing now with 3 weeks remaining gives teams time to build the habit

Activity Name: Module: AI as Quality Reviewer
Notes: Deepening AI skills for the refinement and integration phases; using AI to review your own draft for consistency, logical gaps, tone mismatches, and reader confusion; distinct from Week 9's actionability test (which was content-specific) — this is about using AI as a fresh-eyes reader of the full document; prompt strategies: ask AI to read your document as someone unfamiliar with the project and flag where they get lost; builds on the AI skill progression (policy → research prompting → Socratic partner → production partner → quality reviewer); final AI skill-building module before students apply these skills independently through Weeks 11–15

Activity Name: Team sync — review Check-In #2 feedback action items, assess deliverable status, plan integration work
Notes: Primary coordination meeting; teams revisit Check-In #2 debrief notes (9.10) and team processing agreement (9.06) to check: what feedback has been addressed, what remains, what's the plan for assembling a complete package; heavier than 30-min check-in because conversation requires both backward review and forward planning across the Development-to-Refinement transition

Activity Name: Continue project production — integration, section development, and gap-filling
Notes: Largest production block; with reflection moved to Week 9, this week has more room for integration work; emphasis shifts from drafting new content to integrating existing sections into a coherent whole; teams address gaps identified in internal review gate (9.07) and incorporate Check-In #2 feedback; applies integration skills from module 9.02

Activity Name: Internal review gate — team reads current draft end-to-end, identifies remaining gaps before Refinement phase
Notes: Second instance of internal review gate (introduced in 9.07); shorter than Week 9 (30 vs 45 min) because teams have done this once before; this instance focuses on identifying what needs to happen in Weeks 11-12 to get the package to near-final state; feeds into Week 11 prep

Activity Name: Review Reflection #4 prompt and Final Deliverables rubric
Notes: Forward prep for Refinement phase; reading Reflection #4 prompt now (due Week 12) helps students start noticing the gap between plan and reality the prompt asks about; reviewing Final Deliverables rubric gives teams a concrete standard for what near-final means entering Week 11; scoped as preview only

Activity Name: Async team communication across the week
Notes: Coordination, logistics, check-ins outside structured activities; includes standing prompts: Weekly Progress Check-In (continues from Week 7), Status Update to Employer (between-reviews update appropriate since the Near-Final Review isn't until Week 11), and Change Log entry (first instance of new habit from 10.04); contribution record relevant to Professional Engagement

Activity Name: Week 11 Overview — objectives, what's happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: First week of Refinement phase; names Near-Final Review and Near-Final Package checkpoint as the week's anchor; orients students to the shift from building to polishing; this is a document-sharing meeting, not a formal presentation — only Weeks 5 and 15 involve formal presentations; flags that the Final Deliverables rubric (previewed in 10.08) is the standard the package will be evaluated against in Week 13

Activity Name: Module: What Does "Quality" Mean?
Notes: The difference between "done" and "polished" in a professional context; what employers notice — formatting consistency, error-free prose, visual coherence, logical flow; connects to the Deliverable Quality dimension on the Final Deliverables rubric (30% of grade); builds on the quality orientation seeded in Week 7 (7.03 — From Plan to Product) but shifts focus from completeness to craft

Activity Name: Module: Self-Review Before Client Review
Notes: A practical self-review protocol: read the full document as if you've never seen it, check alignment with the project brief and employer feedback, look for the errors that hide in plain sight (inconsistent terminology, orphaned references, formatting drift across sections); positions self-review as a professional habit, not a last-minute scramble; directly prepares students for the internal review gate (11.07)

Activity Name: Module: Preparing for the Near-Final Review
Notes: At this stage, students need different feedback than in earlier reviews; Weeks 7 and 9 check-ins were question-driven — Week 11 shifts to sharing the actual deliverable package for employer review; how to prepare the document for a pre-read or in-meeting review rather than a formal presentation; how to frame targeted questions about quality, completeness, and remaining gaps; includes guidance on presenting the Change Log to demonstrate responsiveness to prior feedback

Activity Name: Team sync — review deliverable status, assign refinement priorities, plan Near-Final Review preparation
Notes: Primary coordination meeting for the Refinement phase; teams assess the current state of the package against the Final Deliverables rubric dimensions (Deliverable Quality, Analytical Rigor, Actionability, Communication, Responsiveness to Feedback); identify the highest-priority gaps and assign refinement work; heavier than a check-in because this is the first meeting where quality — not content — drives the agenda

Activity Name: Continue project production — refinement, polish, integration, and gap-filling
Notes: Largest production block; emphasis shifts from drafting new content to refining existing work; tasks include: tightening prose, ensuring consistency across sections, strengthening weak arguments, improving visual presentation, verifying that recommendations are specific and actionable; teams should front-load this work before the internal review gate

Activity Name: Internal review gate — full package read-through before employer sees it
Notes: Third instance of internal review gate (introduced 9.07, repeated 10.07); shorter than Week 9 because teams have established the practice; this instance focuses on quality and presentation rather than structural gaps — does the package read as a professional document the team would be proud to hand to the employer?

Activity Name: Team prep session — prepare deliverable package for sharing, develop targeted questions for employer
Notes: Preparation for Near-Final Review; unlike Weeks 5 and 15 (which involve formal presentations), this meeting is document-sharing + questions; teams prepare the package for employer review (clean, complete, navigable), develop targeted questions about quality, completeness, and remaining gaps, and prepare a brief Change Log walkthrough showing how prior feedback was addressed; emphasis on what feedback the team needs now, not on performing progress

Activity Name: Near-Final Review — share deliverable package with employer, discuss quality and completeness, bring targeted questions
Notes: 60-min hold (may end early); employer reviews deliverable package (pre-read or during meeting); team walks through Change Log briefly, then focuses on targeted questions about quality, completeness, and remaining gaps; no formal presentation — the document speaks for itself; discussion of final presentation format and expectations if time permits; this is the last touchpoint before Final Check-In in Week 13

Activity Name: Post-meeting debrief — capture Near-Final Review feedback, update Change Log, identify final revision priorities
Notes: Immediate team debrief; parallels debrief pattern established in Weeks 5, 7, and 9; critical to capture feedback while fresh; Change Log update is now a standing practice (introduced 10.04); this debrief feeds directly into the final production push in Weeks 12–13

Activity Name: Finalize and submit Near-Final Package checkpoint
Notes: Team submits the assembled near-final deliverable package; completion-based checkpoint — package should represent substantially complete work that has been through internal review and is ready for employer scrutiny; this is not a final draft but should be close enough that the Near-Final Review feedback addresses quality, not fundamentals; AI disclosure required

Activity Name: Async team communication across the week
Notes: Coordination, logistics, check-ins outside structured activities; includes standing prompts: Weekly Progress Check-In (continues from Week 7), Status Update to Employer (between-reviews coordination as needed), and Change Log entry; contribution record relevant to Professional Engagement and Peer Evaluation #2 in Week 15

Activity Name: Week 12 Overview — objectives, what's happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: Names Reflection #4, deliverable finalization, and presentation planning as this week's priorities; dual focus: finishing the deliverable and beginning to think about how to present it; Presentation Fundamentals module and Draft Presentation Outline moved here from Week 14 to create a two-week presentation arc (conceptual/planning in Week 12, operational/build in Week 14); includes new Approaching Reflection #4 scaffolding module; flags Final Deliverables submission in Week 13; no employer touchpoint this week

Activity Name: Module: Final Iteration — Incorporating Feedback and Knowing When to Stop
Notes: How to apply near-final feedback without destabilizing work that's already good; the diminishing returns of revision; when 'good enough' is the professional call; directly feeds the post-Near-Final-Review revision work this week; distinct from Week 11 quality content — Week 11 was about what quality means, this is about knowing when to stop revising

Activity Name: Module: Approaching Reflection #4
Notes: Brief scaffolding for the most analytically demanding reflection in the portfolio; what makes Reflection #4 different from #1–3: synthesizing across a longer arc (Weeks 10–12), examining the gap between the plan made in Week 4 and the work actually produced; how to select an anchor experience from 6 weeks of development and refinement rather than defaulting to the most recent event; the difference between describing what changed and examining why it changed and what that reveals; parallels the structure of Week 14's 'Approaching the Synthesis Reflection' module for #5; positioned before the reflection drafting activity (12.05)

Activity Name: Module: Presentation Fundamentals
Notes: Structure, visual design basics, speaking to clients vs. academic audiences; moved to Week 12 from Week 14 to give students a two-week presentation arc rather than a one-week sprint; students encounter the conceptual foundations of effective presentations while still finalizing the deliverable — presentation planning often reveals gaps in the deliverable itself; the Final Presentation (Week 15) is one of only two formal presentations in the course; Direction Check (Week 5) was scaffolded as a validation meeting, not formal presentation skill-building

Activity Name: Team sync — review Near-Final Review feedback, plan final revisions and presentation work
Notes: Primary coordination meeting; teams review employer feedback from Week 11 debrief (11.10), prioritize final revisions, and begin dividing presentation responsibilities; heavier than a check-in because this week splits attention between deliverable finalization and presentation prep

Activity Name: Apply Near-Final Review feedback and finalize deliverables
Notes: Final production push; teams apply employer feedback from the Near-Final Review, finalize all deliverable components, ensure Change Log is complete, and prepare the package for Week 13 submission; emphasis on targeted revision, not new content; trimmed from 225 to 195 min to accommodate Presentation Fundamentals module and Draft Outline moved into this week from Week 14; the presentation outline work (12.07) partly overlaps with this production work since organizing a presentation often clarifies the deliverable itself

Activity Name: Draft Presentation Outline
Notes: Teams develop the narrative arc, assign presentation roles, plan visual support, and outline the Q&A strategy; moved to Week 12 from Week 14 to give students time to plan before build week; feeds from Presentation Fundamentals module (12.06); this is a first draft — teams will refine in Week 14 when they build the deck; starting the outline now means presentation thinking can inform the final deliverable polish

Activity Name: Reflection #4 — draft and submit (DEAL model)
Notes: Fourth DEAL reflection; examines the gap between what was planned and what was produced — what changed in the student's understanding of the problem, the deliverables, or their own capabilities; targets Career & Self-Development (reflection, growth orientation) and Critical Thinking (evidence-based reasoning); previewed in Week 10 (10.08); AI-as-Socratic-partner strategy applies; AI-assisted drafting is off-limits for reflections

Activity Name: Async team communication across the week
Notes: Coordination, logistics, check-ins outside structured activities; includes standing prompts: Weekly Progress Check-In, Status Update to Employer (as needed before Final Check-In in Week 13), and Change Log entry (final updates before submission); contribution record relevant to Peer Evaluation #2 in Week 15

Activity Name: Week 13 Overview — objectives, what's happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: Names Final Deliverables submission as the week's anchor; Final Check-In with employer available if team has substantive questions but should be cancelled if not needed — async communication about Week 15 presentation logistics either way; orients to this being primarily a submission week with minimal module content

Activity Name: Module: Final Deliverable Checklist
Notes: Pre-submission review checklist: formatting consistency, Change Log completeness, alignment with project brief and rubric dimensions, AI disclosure included; practical, not conceptual — students run through this before hitting submit

Activity Name: Module: The Home Stretch
Notes: Brief mindset content — finishing strong, managing the last-minute impulse to overhaul, trusting the work you've done; short because students don't need a lecture at this point

Activity Name: Team sync — review submission readiness, decide on Final Check-In, coordinate presentation logistics
Notes: Lighter coordination meeting; teams decide whether to hold or cancel the Final Check-In based on whether they have substantive questions; review deliverable status against rubric; coordinate async communication to employer about Week 15 presentation logistics (format, timing, attendees)

Activity Name: Final Check-In — available if team has remaining questions; cancel if not needed; async communication about Week 15 presentation logistics
Notes: 60-min hold; teams should cancel if they don't have substantive questions for the employer; if held: brief status update + remaining questions + confirm presentation logistics; if cancelled: team sends async communication to employer confirming Week 15 presentation format, timing, and attendees; this is the final employer touchpoint before the Final Presentation

Activity Name: Final revisions and deliverable assembly
Notes: Final production block; teams make any last adjustments, run through the Final Deliverable Checklist (13.02), ensure all components are assembled (deliverable package + Change Log + AI disclosure), and prepare for submission; if Final Check-In was held, incorporate any last input

Activity Name: Internal review gate — final end-to-end read before submission
Notes: Fourth and final instance of internal review gate (introduced 9.07); last quality check before the highest-stakes submission of the course; teams read the complete package one final time with the rubric in hand

Activity Name: Finalize and submit Final Deliverables (graded)
Notes: Team submits the complete deliverable package including all deliverable components, Change Log, and AI disclosure; rubric-based grading across five dimensions (Deliverable Quality 30%, Analytical Rigor 25%, Actionability 20%, Communication 15%, Responsiveness to Feedback 10%); this is 30% of the course grade

Activity Name: Preview Reflection #5 prompt and Peer Evaluation #2 instructions
Notes: Forward prep for Weeks 14–15; reading both prompts now helps students begin noticing what they'll reflect on and start thinking about teammate contributions; explicitly signals that starting early on both is encouraged in Week 14

Activity Name: Async team communication across the week
Notes: Coordination, logistics, check-ins outside structured activities; includes employer communication about Week 15 presentation logistics if Final Check-In is cancelled; final week of Weekly Progress Check-In standing prompt; contribution record relevant to Peer Evaluation #2

Activity Name: Week 14 Overview — objectives, what's happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: First week of Integration phase; Presentation Fundamentals and Draft Outline were completed in Week 12 — this week focuses on operational planning (Planning Your Presentation, Handling Q&A), finalizing the Presentation Outline checkpoint, building the deck, and rehearsing; also includes Transfer module and scaffolding for Reflection #5 and Peer Eval #2, both of which students begin this week and finalize in Week 15; the two-week presentation arc (conceptual in Week 12, operational in Week 14) gives students time to absorb and plan before building; recommended completion order: planning and Q&A modules first, then finalize outline, then build deck, then rehearse, then begin reflection and peer eval
Week Title: Preparing to Present and Looking Ahead

Activity Name: Module: Planning Your Presentation
Notes: Who presents what, handling team dynamics during the presentation, practicing effectively; practical planning that feeds directly into the Presentation Outline finalization and deck build this week; how to structure a narrative arc for the employer audience; distinct from Presentation Fundamentals (now in Week 12) — this is about the logistics and strategy of presenting as a team; positioned after Handling Q&A (14.03) so teams can incorporate Q&A prep into their planning
Week Title: Preparing to Present and Looking Ahead

Activity Name: Module: Handling Q&A
Notes: How to respond to questions you can't fully answer; thinking on your feet without fabricating; the defend-your-work standard — the employer and instructor will ask about your findings and recommendations, and your answers need to come from your own understanding; how to say 'I don't know but here's how I'd find out'; handling pushback on recommendations gracefully; directly prepares for the Q&A portion of the Final Presentation (Week 15) and the rehearsal (14.09); this is the skill that distinguishes students who genuinely engaged with the work from those who outsourced the thinking
Week Title: Preparing to Present and Looking Ahead

Activity Name: Module: Transfer — Taking It With You
Notes: How to extract transferable principles from the project experience; the difference between 'I learned about credit unions' and 'I learned how to translate ambiguous client needs into a research plan'; connects to Reflection #5 prompt (which asks for the single most important thing learned); builds on Week 10 modules (10.02, 10.03) which helped students notice tacit learning — this module is about articulating and packaging it
Week Title: Preparing to Present and Looking Ahead

Activity Name: Finalize and submit Presentation Outline checkpoint
Notes: Team submits the finalized Presentation Outline; completion-based checkpoint; outline should include narrative structure, role assignments, key talking points per section, and planned visual support; drafted in Week 12 (12.07), refined and submitted here before build begins; AI disclosure required
Week Title: Preparing to Present and Looking Ahead

Activity Name: Module: Approaching the Synthesis Reflection
Notes: Brief scaffolding for Reflection #5 — the most demanding reflection in the portfolio; how to select an anchor experience from 15 weeks rather than defaulting to the most recent one; the difference between summarizing what happened and identifying what changed in how you work professionally; positioned before students begin drafting (14.09); complements the Transfer module (14.04) which provides the conceptual framework
Week Title: Preparing to Present and Looking Ahead

Activity Name: Module: Approaching the Final Peer Evaluation
Notes: What's different about evaluating the full project arc vs. the mid-project evaluation in Week 8; how to assess contributions that evolved over time; the weight this evaluation carries in the Professional Engagement grade; how to write feedback that's useful to a teammate entering their next professional experience; positioned before students begin the evaluation (14.10)
Week Title: Preparing to Present and Looking Ahead

Activity Name: Build presentation — slides, visual materials, speaker notes
Notes: Teams build the actual presentation materials based on the Presentation Outline; includes slides, visual support (charts, diagrams, key data points from the deliverable), and speaker notes; the content already exists in the Final Deliverables — this is about translating document content into presentation format; 150 min reflects that even with existing content, building a coherent slide deck takes real time for students who haven't done this before; sequenced after the outline (14.07/14.08) and before rehearsal (14.10)
Week Title: Preparing to Present and Looking Ahead

Activity Name: Presentation rehearsal — run through presentation, give and receive peer feedback
Notes: First full run-through of the presentation as a team; emphasis on timing, transitions between speakers, handling the narrative arc, and anticipating questions; peer feedback within the team on clarity and delivery; 90 min allows for a full run-through plus time to discuss what worked, what didn't, and make adjustments; sequenced after building the presentation (14.09)
Week Title: Preparing to Present and Looking Ahead

Activity Name: Begin Reflection #5 — review prompt, identify anchor experience(s), draft initial DEAL response
Notes: Students begin the most demanding reflection of the course; the prompt asks them to step back over the full 15-week arc and identify the single most important thing learned about how they work professionally; starting now (rather than cramming in Week 15) gives students time to think; previewed in Week 13 (13.09); AI-as-Socratic-partner strategy applies; AI-assisted drafting is off-limits for reflections
Week Title: Preparing to Present and Looking Ahead

Activity Name: Begin Peer Evaluation #2 — review instructions, reflect on teammate contributions across the full project
Notes: Students start the final peer evaluation; unlike Peer Eval #1 (Week 8, which covered Weeks 1–8), this covers the full project arc; includes 10 min to revisit the Week 8 peer eval module content (BARS rubric, Continue/Start/Adjust framework, calibration examples) before beginning; reviewing instructions now and beginning to think through teammate contributions ensures Week 15 isn't overwhelmed; contributes to Professional Engagement grade; previewed in Week 13 (13.09)
Week Title: Preparing to Present and Looking Ahead

Activity Name: Async team communication across the week
Notes: Coordination, logistics, check-ins outside structured activities; final async communication before Week 15; contribution record relevant to Peer Evaluation #2
Week Title: Preparing to Present and Looking Ahead

Activity Name: Week 15 Overview — objectives, what's happening this week, suggested order of completion
Notes: Final week of the course; names Final Presentation, Reflection #5, and Peer Eval #2 as this week's deliverables; orients to the sequence: present, close with your team, reflect, evaluate teammates; flags that Reflection #5 and Peer Eval #2 were started in Week 14 and should be finalized after the presentation experience

Activity Name: Module: Presentation Day Prep
Notes: Mindset, handling nerves, final run-through tips; brief and practical — students have already rehearsed in Week 14; this is about being ready on the day, not learning new skills; complements the presentation modules from Week 14 (14.02, 14.03)

Activity Name: Module: Articulating Your Experience
Notes: How to talk about this project in interviews, on resumes, and on LinkedIn; what employers want to hear vs. what students tend to say; framing project contributions as professional accomplishments; concrete examples of before/after resume bullets and interview answers; includes guidance on using AI to refine professional framing — prompting AI to strengthen resume bullets, practice interview responses, and identify which accomplishments to lead with; distinct from AI-assisted reflection (which is off-limits) because this is about packaging and communicating learning, not generating the learning itself; serves Career & Self-Development (Career Articulation) subskill; positioned after the Final Presentation and alongside Reflection #5 so students can translate what they've just articulated into professional framing

Activity Name: Final presentation polish — last adjustments to slides, practice transitions, prep for Q&A
Notes: Final team preparation before the presentation; not a full rehearsal (that was Week 14) — this is targeted adjustments based on what the rehearsal surfaced; practicing opening and closing, confirming timing, reviewing anticipated questions

Activity Name: Final Presentation — present completed work to employer and course supervisor
Notes: 60-min hold; formal presentation of completed work — narrative, findings, recommendations, implications (20–25 min); employer questions (15 min); employer feedback and reflections on the work (10 min); wrap-up, deliverable handoff, acknowledgments, closure (5–10 min); this is the second of only two formal student presentations in the course (the other was the Direction Check in Week 5) and the culmination of 15 weeks of work

Activity Name: Post-presentation debrief and team closing — capture employer feedback, acknowledge team contributions, close the experience
Notes: Two parts: (1) capture employer feedback from the presentation while fresh; (2) structured team closing — what we accomplished together, what we learned from each other, individual acknowledgments; this is the last time the team works together as a team; giving the experience a proper ending matters for both closure and for feeding Reflection #5; the closing conversation may surface insights students want to incorporate into their final reflection

Activity Name: Finalize and submit Reflection #5 (graded)
Notes: Finalizes the reflection begun in Week 14 (14.09); the presentation, employer feedback, and team closing may surface final insights to incorporate; examines the full 15-week arc — the single most important thing learned about how the student works professionally; the most demanding reflection in the portfolio; AI-as-Socratic-partner strategy applies; AI-assisted drafting is off-limits; ~5% of course grade

Activity Name: Finalize and submit Peer Evaluation #2
Notes: Finalizes the peer evaluation begun in Week 14 (14.10); covers the full project arc (Weeks 1–15); includes qualitative feedback (Continue/Start/Adjust), BARS ratings, and self-evaluation for each of 3 teammates; approximately 45–50 min per person to do thoughtfully; contributes to Professional Engagement grade, not graded standalone

Activity Name: Async team communication across the week
Notes: Final coordination; includes any follow-up on deliverable handoff to employer; last contribution record entry of the course
